findings.title: Sitemap and page canonicals point at .html URLs that 308-redirect, weakening the discovery flywheel that is the owner's #1 ask
findings.file: site/sitemap.xml (lines 9-62); site/guide-*.html + site/faq.html + site/compare-vs-diy.html <link rel=canonical>; site/docs.html, site/pricing.html, site/compare.html, site/aup.html, site/privacy.html, site/terms.html (no canonical at all)
findings.failure_scenario: Cloudflare Pages serves every page at a clean URL (/docs, /pricing, /guide-cold-email-with-ai-agent) and 308-redirects the .html form to it (verified live: /pricing.html->308->/pricing, /guide-cold-email-with-ai-agent.html->308->clean, /compare-vs-diy.html->308->clean). But (a) all 11 non-root sitemap.xml <loc> entries are the .html (redirecting) URLs, and (b) the pages that DO carry a canonical point it at their own .html URL — verified live, the 200 page at /guide-cold-email-with-ai-agent serves `<link rel=canonical href=...guide-cold-email-with-ai-agent.html>`, i.e. a canonical that points at a redirect back to itself — while docs/pricing/compare/legal carry no canonical at all. A search or AI crawler indexing the AEO surface gets a sitemap full of redirects plus self-conflicting/absent canonical signals, wastes crawl budget on redirect hops, and may consolidate signals onto the wrong URL. For a brand-new zero-authority site whose entire value proposition is 'agents/search discover and recommend us,' this directly dilutes the flywheel.
findings.fix: Make every sitemap.xml <loc> the clean 200 URL (drop the .html). Change every existing `<link rel=canonical>` to the clean URL and ADD a self-referencing clean-URL canonical to the pages missing one (docs, pricing, compare, aup, privacy, terms). Also switch internal nav/footer hrefs and llms.txt links from `foo.html` to `foo` so crawlers and agents never traverse a 308. Re-verify with `curl -sI` that each sitemap URL returns 200 and each served canonical matches the fetched URL.

findings.title: Waitlist leads silently expire after 90 days and have zero owner-facing retrieval, so the site's 'get notified when real sending goes live' promise can't be kept for early signups
findings.file: apps/platform/src/routes/waitlist.ts:21 (EMAIL_KEY_TTL_SECONDS = 90*24*60*60) and :66 (KV put with that TTL); no reader in apps/platform/src/routes/admin-ops.ts or apps/platform/src/engine/ops-summary.ts
findings.failure_scenario: The marketing site's only lead-capture surface (index.html #waitlist -> POST /api/waitlist) stores each email in KV under key `email:<addr>` with a 90-day expirationTtl. Activation is owner-hands and the stated GTM plan is to let the site age/index (readily >90 days to launch), so the earliest and most-interested signups are auto-deleted before real sending ever goes live. Worse, nothing reads the waitlist back: grep confirms WAITLIST KV is only written, never read for retrieval; buildOpsDigest and every admin route ignore it, so the owner cannot see the waitlist count or export addresses without manual `wrangler kv key list/get`. Net: index.html promises 'Get notified when real sending goes live' / 'We'll only email you about this project', but the system structurally forgets its leads and gives the owner no way to reach them — the top of the self-running funnel silently drops to empty.
findings.fix: Separate the durable email store from the 60-120s rate-limit bucket: persist waitlist emails without a 90-day TTL (unexpiring KV entry, or a D1 `waitlist` table which also supports ordered export). Add owner visibility — either an ADMIN_TOKEN-gated `GET /admin/ops/waitlist` (list/count) or a `waitlist: {count}` field in buildOpsDigest — so the count shows up in the digest and the addresses are exportable at activation. Keep the short TTL only on the `rl:` rate-limit keys.

findings.title: openapi.yaml and the live MCP tools/list disagree on which launch_campaign fields are required — an agent cross-referencing the two published contracts gets contradictory schemas
findings.file: site/openapi.yaml:389-393 (LeadInput required [email,firstName], company default) & :397,411-418 (LaunchCampaignInput required [name,offer,leads,sequence]; timezone/sendWindow/stopOnReply have defaults); vs live /mcp tools/list generated at apps/platform/src/mcp/handler.ts:88 (z.toJSONSchema) over the defaulted zod in packages/shared/src/intents.ts:35-47
findings.failure_scenario: llms.txt presents openapi.yaml as the 'exact request/response schemas' and guide-mcp-cold-email.html claims its schemas are 'verified against the live tools/list response' — but the two contracts contradict each other. openapi marks `company`, `timezone`, `sendWindow`, and `stopOnReply` optional (defaults); the live MCP tools/list marks all four REQUIRED (Zod v4 z.toJSONSchema emits defaulted fields into `required` under output mode). Verified live that runtime is permissive on both surfaces (a launch_campaign / POST /campaigns omitting all four succeeds), so there is no hard failure — but an agent that strictly validates its tool call against the advertised MCP inputSchema is forced to fabricate a `company`, `sendWindow`, and `timezone` it was told are mandatory, while an agent trusting openapi omits them; the two paths behave inconsistently and the docs can't both be right.
findings.fix: Regenerate the MCP inputSchema in input/parse mode so fields carrying a zod `.default()` are emitted as optional (not in `required`), matching openapi.yaml and actual runtime — e.g. pass the input-perspective option to z.toJSONSchema (or drop the defaults and validate-then-default). Then re-verify tools/list `required` arrays equal the openapi `required` arrays for launch_campaign/leads, and re-derive guide-mcp-cold-email.html from the corrected output.
